news_summary: Recent scientific advances reveal progress in East Asian research competitiveness, new insights into multicellularity and animal origins, preservation of ancient DNA through frozen specimens, and discoveries on pigeons' magnetic navigation mechanisms.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées scientifiques mettent en lumière les progrès réalisés en matière de compétitivité de la recherche en Asie de l'Est, apportent de nouvelles perspectives sur la multicellularité et les origines du monde animal, révèlent comment l'ADN ancien a pu être préservé grâce à des spécimens congelés, et font la lumière sur les mécanismes de navigation magnétique des pigeons.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances científicos ponen de manifiesto el progreso en la competitividad investigadora de Asia Oriental, nuevos conocimientos sobre la multicelularidad y los orígenes de los animales, la conservación del ADN antiguo a través de especímenes congelados y descubrimientos sobre los mecanismos de navegación magnética de las palomas.

articles.title: Nature Index 2026 Research Leaders rankings: are China’s East Asian neighbours keeping pace with it?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 10 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01662-xJapan and South Korea are challenging Western peers in a shifting research landscape.
articles.content:
An engineer at Japan-based robotics firm Enactic uses a virtual-reality headset to test robotic arms in the company’s office in Tokyo.Credit: Philip Fong/AFP via Getty

China has continued its steep rise in research output, according to Nature Index’s annual rankings, but there is growing evidence that other East Asian countries are also challenging major research nations in Europe and North America.

The Research Leaders tables rank countries and territories according to their contributions to articles published in journals tracked by Nature Index. They show that China’s contribution rose by 22% between 2024 and 2025, far ahead of the rest of the top ten, to extend the country’s lead at the top of the ranking.

Japan and South Korea, which are ranked fifth and seventh, respectively, each posted an increase of almost 10% growth in output according to a metric called Share, which tracks author affiliations on research articles. That’s a bigger increase than higher-ranked Western peers such as the United States, Germany and the United Kingdom (see ‘Relative measures’).

This year’s ranking marks a watershed for the Nature Index, which for the first time has integrated a set of applied-sciences journals into the database, amounting to about 20,000 extra articles in 2025. A number of social-science journals have also been added, representing about 2,000 extra articles. Furthermore, the database has switched to categorizing research disciplines at the article level, rather than by journal, to better reflect the subject mix found in journals. To ensure that year-on-year performance is tracked fairly, however, data from both 2024 and 2025 have been added, and output increases have been calculated against the updated figures.

Mission critical

Increases in Share from 2024 to 2025 should be assessed against the year-on-year growth in the total number of articles in the database. This comes out at 11%, so Japan and South Korea, which each had an increase in Share that was slightly lower than this, lost a little ground to China. Adjusted Share, a metric that accounts for changes in database size, fell by 1% for South Korea and 2% for Japan. But the figures still point to these countries performing much better than large research systems in Europe and North America did: adjusted Share dropped by 6% for the United States and by 7% for Germany and the United Kingdom. Some major Western research institutions also had large drops in adjusted Share from 2024 to 2025, including Harvard University in Cambridge, Massachusetts, which lost its position as the leading university for Nature Index output to Zhejiang University in Hangzhou, China.

Nature Index 2026 Research Leaders

For Japan, the encouraging numbers will raise a hopeful question. Is the country’s often-discussed research malaise — represented by sharp falls in its adjusted Share in the Nature Index from 2015 to the early 2020s and concerns over cuts to university funding — beginning to ease? Motoko Kotani, adviser to the president of Tohoku University in Sendai, Japan, says that the numbers do suggest that Japan is adapting to a global research environment that is increasingly tilted towards interdisciplinary, mission-led and computational work.

Kotani traces some of that shift back to a change about ten years ago in how Japanese policymakers framed research. Up to that point, Japan’s science system was often described as siloed and slow to adapt, with universities operating in highly specialized academic enclaves that did not collaborate enough with industry. In the mid-2010s, Kotani says, policymakers started asking what science was really for. “The answer wasn’t scientists,” she says. “It was society.” Once science is framed that way, interdisciplinarity follows, she argues, because “you cannot solve society’s problems with only one discipline”.

These policy conversations were followed by reforms such as stronger backing for strategically selected universities, greater institutional autonomy and better support for early-career researchers, according to Kotani. In 2017, for example, the government rolled out its Designated National University Corporation System, which selects a small number of universities deemed capable of rivalling what the government calls the “world’s top-level” research institutions. These universities are granted freedoms from certain regulations, so that they can establish spin-off companies and offer increased salaries to attract foreign researchers.

All of this has been reinforced with significant government spending. In 2023, the government created a ¥10-trillion endowment fund (worth US$63 billion today) — its most ambitious academic investment in decades. The move aimed to emulate the US model, in which large university endowments are invested to generate steady returns that support research in the long run. A year later, Tohoku University became the first recipient of funding from the endowment pot.

Japan still has hurdles to clear. “Our challenge remains internationalization,” says Kotani. The country’s academia is still wrestling with how to make universities less insular, more attractive to foreign researchers and more open to collaboration with institutions overseas.

The government hopes that the Japan Science and Technology Agency’s Adopting Sustainable Partnerships for Innovative Research Ecosystem (ASPIRE) programme — launched in 2023 to strengthen international collaboration — will help. Under ASPIRE, “top scientists” can apply for up to ¥500 million for projects that involve partners based in a select number of countries, in strategically chosen research areas such as artificial intelligence, biotechnology and semiconductors.

Ecosystem effect

South Korea’s biggest Share jump came in applied sciences, in which its score rose by 14% from 2024 to 2025; its natural-sciences performance was strong, too, rising by 11%.

Ramón Pacheco Pardo, a researcher in international relations at King’s College London and the institution’s regional envoy for East and southeast Asia, says that South Korea’s success cannot be traced to one ministry, company or subfield. “It’s the whole ecosystem,” he says. “South Korea’s research system has long been tied to its industrial strategy and bolstered by its advanced manufacturing and technology-heavy exports.”

That opinion is echoed by So Young Kim, a science and technology policy researcher at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology in Daejeon. “This approach is historically rooted in the country’s industrial policy during its development decades. What we’re seeing now is the global revival of industrial policy,” she says.

In 2023, South Korea spent close to 5% of its entire economic output on research and development — second only to Israel among members of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD). But a high proportion of this investment takes place in private companies: 81% of South Korea’s research spending in 2024 was in the business sector, according to OECD data.

The government says that the country’s total 2025 research budget was more than 16% higher than that in 2024. Like Japan, South Korea has chosen to home in on specific research fields: the government wants 44% of its research budget to be directed towards a ‘pioneering’ research approach that focuses on technologies that it calls game-changing, such as AI and quantum technology.

Humanoid robots being trained at the headquarters of South Korean electronics firm LG in Seoul.Credit: SeongJoon Cho/Bloomberg via Getty

South Korea’s method of combining private and public research means it is especially well aligned with the direction of global science, says Pacheco Pardo. In semiconductors, robotics and AI, research is not happening in a vacuum in the country: it sits in a well-funded industrial base that can absorb it, scale it up and pay for more of it, he argues. This matters not just at the level of national strategy and funding, but also in terms of attracting the best scientific talent, from both inside and outside the system.

Still, the South Korean system has gaps. Pacheco Pardo argues that it remains less dominant in fundamental research than China, Europe, Japan and the United States. Its latest ranking in the Nature Index perhaps reflects this: South Korea places eighth in natural sciences and third in applied sciences (see ‘Subject specialists’). This gap is one reason, he says, that South Korean institutions are increasingly considering partnering with firms from countries where fundamental research is a strength.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant positive developments in research output and innovation capacity in East Asian countries, particularly China, Japan, and South Korea, highlighting their growing contributions to global science and technology. It details policy reforms, increased funding, and strategic focus on interdisciplinary and applied sciences, which have broad societal and economic implications. The story is focused, substantive, and reflects meaningful progress in the global research landscape.
articles.personality_title: East Asian countries boost research output, challenging Western leaders
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – China has been leading a big rise in scientific research, but now its neighbors Japan and South Korea are also growing their contributions to global science. The Nature Index ranks countries by the research papers they publish in important journals.

**What happened** – In 2025, China increased its research output by 22%, staying in first place. Japan and South Korea, ranked fifth and seventh, each grew their research output by about 10%. This growth is faster than that of top Western countries like the US, Germany, and the UK. The Nature Index expanded its data this year to include more applied sciences and social sciences, giving a clearer picture of research trends.

**Impact** – Japan’s rise shows signs that it is overcoming past problems like low funding and slow adaptation. It has changed policies to focus on research that solves real-world problems and supports universities with more freedom and money. South Korea’s success comes from linking research closely with its strong industry, spending heavily on research and development, mostly through private companies. Both countries focus on important future technologies like artificial intelligence and semiconductors.

**What’s next step** – Japan aims to attract more foreign researchers and improve international collaboration through new programs like ASPIRE. South Korea plans to continue investing in pioneering research areas and build partnerships with countries strong in fundamental science. Together, these efforts could help East Asia play an even bigger role in global science and technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Japan and South Korea are rapidly increasing their scientific research, narrowing the gap with China and challenging Western countries by focusing on applied science and innovation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pays d’Asie de l’Est renforcent leur recherche, défiant les leaders occidentaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Chine mène une forte progression dans la recherche scientifique, mais ses voisins, le Japon et la Corée du Sud, augmentent aussi leurs contributions à la science mondiale. L’Indice Nature classe les pays selon les articles scientifiques publiés dans des revues importantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, la Chine a augmenté sa production de recherche de 22 %, restant en tête. Le Japon et la Corée du Sud, classés cinquième et septième, ont chacun augmenté leur production d’environ 10 %. Cette croissance est plus rapide que celle des principaux pays occidentaux comme les États-Unis, l’Allemagne et le Royaume-Uni. L’Indice Nature a élargi ses données cette année pour inclure plus de sciences appliquées et sociales, offrant une vision plus complète.

**Impact** – La montée du Japon montre des signes de dépassement de problèmes passés comme le faible financement et la lente adaptation. Il a changé ses politiques pour se concentrer sur la recherche qui résout des problèmes concrets et soutient les universités avec plus de liberté et de ressources. Le succès de la Corée du Sud vient de sa liaison étroite entre recherche et industrie, avec de fortes dépenses en recherche et développement, principalement via des entreprises privées. Les deux pays ciblent des technologies clés comme l’intelligence artificielle et les semi-conducteurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Japon veut attirer plus de chercheurs étrangers et améliorer la collaboration internationale grâce à des programmes comme ASPIRE. La Corée du Sud prévoit de continuer à investir dans la recherche pionnière et à établir des partenariats avec des pays forts en recherche fondamentale. Ces efforts pourraient renforcer le rôle de l’Asie de l’Est dans la science et la technologie mondiales.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Japon et la Corée du Sud augmentent rapidement leur recherche scientifique, réduisant l’écart avec la Chine et défiant les pays occidentaux grâce à leur focus sur les sciences appliquées et l’innovation.
articles.personality_title_es: Países de Asia Oriental aumentan su investigación, desafiando a líderes occidentales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – China ha liderado un gran aumento en la investigación científica, pero ahora sus vecinos Japón y Corea del Sur también están aumentando sus aportes a la ciencia mundial. El Índice Nature clasifica a los países según los artículos científicos publicados en revistas importantes.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, China incrementó su producción de investigación en un 22 %, manteniéndose en primer lugar. Japón y Corea del Sur, en quinto y séptimo lugar respectivamente, aumentaron su producción en cerca del 10 %. Este crecimiento es más rápido que el de los principales países occidentales como Estados Unidos, Alemania y Reino Unido. El Índice Nature amplió sus datos este año para incluir más ciencias aplicadas y sociales, mostrando una imagen más completa.

**Impacto** – El avance de Japón muestra señales de superar problemas pasados como el bajo financiamiento y la lenta adaptación. Cambió sus políticas para enfocarse en la investigación que resuelve problemas reales y apoya a las universidades con más libertad y recursos. El éxito de Corea del Sur se debe a su fuerte conexión entre investigación e industria, con grandes gastos en investigación y desarrollo, principalmente en empresas privadas. Ambos países se centran en tecnologías clave como inteligencia artificial y semiconductores.

**Próximo paso** – Japón busca atraer a más investigadores extranjeros y mejorar la colaboración internacional mediante programas como ASPIRE. Corea del Sur planea seguir invirtiendo en investigación pionera y formar alianzas con países fuertes en investigación fundamental. Estos esfuerzos podrían aumentar el papel de Asia Oriental en la ciencia y tecnología global.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Japón y Corea del Sur están aumentando rápidamente su investigación científica, reduciendo la brecha con China y desafiando a los países occidentales con su enfoque en ciencias aplicadas e innovación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of three interconnected robotic arms—one styled with Japanese design motifs, another with South Korean technological patterns, and the third with Chinese calligraphic accents—working together over a glowing, stylized open research journal, all set against a warm, softly lit abstract map of East Asia in natural, muted tones.

articles.title: A unicellular relative links aggregative multicellularity to animal origins
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10748-5A unicellular relative links aggregative multicellularity to animal origins
articles.content: How animals evolved complex multicellularity from their unicellular ancestors remains unanswered. Unicellular relatives of animals exhibit simple multicellularity through clonal division, formation of multinucleate coenocytes, or aggregation. 1 Therefore, animal multicellularity may have evolved from one (or a combination) of these behaviours. Aggregation has classically been dismissed as a means to complex multicellularity. 2 However, aggregation occurs in many extant animal cells and has also been recently described in three close unicellular relatives of animals (the choanoflagellates Salpingoeca rosetta and Choanoeca flexa, and the filasterean Capsaspora owczarzaki). 3-5 It is unclear whether aggregation in these species is derived or ancestral, and its relevance for animal origins remains unknown. To fill this gap, we investigated whether an additional close unicellular relative of animals can undergo aggregation. We discovered that the marine free-living bacterivorous filasterean Ministeria vibrans 6 forms homogeneous aggregates with reproducible kinetics that have long-term stability, and that improved feeding and mating may be evolutionary drivers of this aggregation. Notably, we found that homologs of many animal multicellularity genes involved in cell adhesion, signalling, and transcriptional regulation were deployed during the aggregation process, indicating that they may have been used for aggregation in the unicellular ancestors of animals before being co-opted into animal multicellular development. Thus, our results imply that aggregative multicellularity was key to the development of the multicellular animal genetic toolkit.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the evolutionary origins of animal multicellularity, revealing that aggregative multicellularity in unicellular relatives played a key role. This breakthrough enhances understanding of a fundamental biological process with broad implications for evolutionary biology and related fields, offering meaningful insight into the origins of complex life.
articles.personality_title: New discovery shows how single-celled organisms helped animals become multicellular
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how animals, which are made of many cells, evolved from single-celled ancestors. Some single-celled relatives of animals can stick together in simple ways, but it was unclear if this helped animals become multicellular.

**What happened** – Researchers studied a tiny ocean creature called Ministeria vibrans, which is closely related to animals but lives as a single cell. They found that these cells can join together to form stable groups. During this process, the cells use genes similar to those animals use to stick together and communicate. This suggests that the ability to form groups by sticking together existed before animals evolved.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that forming groups by aggregation was likely a step toward animals becoming multicellular. It also reveals that genes animals use today for making many cells work together were already active in their single-celled ancestors during group formation.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely study other single-celled relatives to see if they also use similar genes and behaviors. Understanding this could help explain how complex animals first evolved and how multicellularity developed over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The study reveals that joining together in groups was an early and important step in the evolution of multicellular animals.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle découverte montre comment les organismes unicellulaires ont aidé les animaux à devenir multicellulaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps comment les animaux, composés de nombreuses cellules, ont évolué à partir d’ancêtres unicellulaires. Certains proches unicellulaires des animaux peuvent s’assembler simplement, mais on ignorait si cela avait aidé les animaux à devenir multicellulaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié une petite créature marine appelée Ministeria vibrans, proche des animaux mais unicellulaire. Ils ont découvert que ces cellules peuvent se regrouper pour former des assemblages stables. Lors de ce processus, elles utilisent des gènes similaires à ceux des animaux pour s’adhérer et communiquer. Cela suggère que la capacité à former des groupes existait avant l’apparition des animaux.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que l’agrégation a probablement été une étape vers la multicellularité animale. Elle révèle aussi que les gènes utilisés aujourd’hui par les animaux pour coordonner plusieurs cellules étaient déjà actifs chez leurs ancêtres unicellulaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement étudier d’autres proches unicellulaires pour voir s’ils utilisent aussi ces gènes et comportements. Cela pourrait aider à comprendre comment les animaux complexes ont évolué et comment la multicellularité s’est développée.

**Message en une phrase** – L’étude montre que le fait de se regrouper était une étape clé dans l’évolution des animaux multicellulaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo descubrimiento muestra cómo organismos unicelulares ayudaron a los animales a volverse multicelulares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo cómo los animales, que tienen muchas células, evolucionaron de ancestros unicelulares. Algunos parientes unicelulares de los animales pueden unirse de formas simples, pero no estaba claro si esto ayudó a los animales a volverse multicelulares.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron una pequeña criatura marina llamada Ministeria vibrans, cercana a los animales pero unicelular. Descubrieron que estas células pueden unirse para formar grupos estables. Durante este proceso, usan genes similares a los que los animales usan para adherirse y comunicarse. Esto sugiere que la capacidad de formar grupos existía antes de que aparecieran los animales.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que formar grupos por agregación probablemente fue un paso hacia que los animales se volvieran multicelulares. También revela que los genes que los animales usan hoy para coordinar muchas células ya estaban activos en sus ancestros unicelulares.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente estudiarán otros parientes unicelulares para ver si también usan genes y comportamientos similares. Esto podría ayudar a explicar cómo evolucionaron los animales complejos y cómo se desarrolló la multicelularidad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El estudio revela que unirse en grupos fue un paso temprano e importante en la evolución de los animales multicelulares.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of delicate, translucent single-celled organisms gently clustering together in a harmonious spiral formation beneath soft ocean waves, with subtle glowing connections symbolizing early cell adhesion and communication, set against a simple, muted background of marine blues and sandy beige.

articles.title: Frozen squirrel scat preserves ancient DNA from hundreds of species
articles.summary: A complex ecosystem of woolly mammoths, bison, horses and big cats has been elucidated by studying the faeces of small rodents that probably ate the bigger animals
articles.content:
A complex ecosystem of woolly mammoths, bison, horses and big cats has been elucidated by studying the faeces of small rodents that probably ate the bigger animals

Arctic ground squirrels gather food from a wide variety of sources and store it in burrows Government of Yukon

A rich and complex ecosystem stretching back 700,000 years that included woolly mammoths, bison, horses and big cats has been unveiled thanks to DNA preserved in frozen faeces.

Arctic ground squirrels (Urocitellus parryii) are rodents about 40 centimetres long, found in cold regions of both North America and Siberia. These areas were joined by a land bridge in the past, with the whole region being known as Beringia.

Advertisement

“The squirrels hibernate for about eight months of the year and in the four months that they’re conscious, they really need to get out there and eat and bring as many resources as they can back to their burrow,” says Tyler Murchie at the Hakai Institute in Campbell River, Canada.

This means their burrows often contain a wealth of faecal pellets and food caches, which makes the animals like “natural archivists”, says Murchie. To see what might be stored in this archive, he and his colleagues looked at preserved faeces – known as coprolites – from 13 Arctic ground squirrel burrows in the central Yukon in Canada that were frozen in permafrost.

The burrows dated to between about 30,000 and 700,000 years ago. From the droppings, each of which is about 1 to 2 centimetres long, the team extracted DNA belonging to a wide range of organisms.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

These include microbes, more than 200 different plant groups and animals including insects, other rodents, woolly mammoths, horses, grey wolves, steppe bison and a big cat that was either an American cheetah or a cougar. “It’s the whole cast of organisms that lived in the Beringian ice-age ecosystem,” says Murchie.

You might assume that ground squirrels would primarily eat nuts and seeds, but that’s not the case, he says. “They’re actually quite omnivorous, almost like little bears. There are reports of ground squirrels eating carcasses of moose and lynx, so the fact that we find all of these large animals in their coprolites isn’t actually that surprising.”

Ancient faecal pellets left by Arctic ground squirrels, found in Yukon, Canada Duane Froese, University of Alberta

Murchie and his colleagues were able to use the DNA they found to reconstruct mitochondrial genomes of many animals from different points in time. These included 12 ground squirrels – one lineage of which dated back 700,000 years – three horses, two bison and one hare. They also found enough DNA to piece together six woolly mammoth genomes, but details of those will be published separately.

“These are fantastically preserved samples that really showcase the ecological diversity of the Yukon through time,” says Kelsey Witt at Clemson University in South Carolina.

She says it is hard to know whether DNA from a given species is present in a coprolite because it was eaten by a ground squirrel, or because it existed in the environment and leached in. But she does say it is feasible that the rodents consumed mammoth meat, given how much DNA was present in the samples and that ground squirrels are often scavengers.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in ancient DNA recovery from frozen squirrel feces, enabling reconstruction of ecosystems from up to 700,000 years ago. This discovery provides valuable insights into prehistoric biodiversity and ecological history, benefiting the broader scientific community and public understanding of natural history.
articles.personality_title: Frozen squirrel droppings reveal 700,000-year-old Arctic ecosystem
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied ancient ecosystems by examining fossils and bones. Recently, researchers discovered that frozen faeces from Arctic ground squirrels can also preserve DNA from many species that lived hundreds of thousands of years ago.

**What happened** – A team led by Tyler Murchie studied frozen droppings, called coprolites, from Arctic ground squirrels found in Yukon, Canada. These burrows were frozen in permafrost and dated from 30,000 to 700,000 years old. By extracting DNA from these small pellets, the scientists identified over 200 plant groups and many animals, including woolly mammoths, horses, bison, wolves, and big cats.

**Impact** – This discovery is unique because it provides a detailed look into a rich Arctic ecosystem from long ago using an unexpected source: squirrel droppings. It shows that these squirrels ate a wide variety of foods, including meat, which helps explain how DNA from large animals ended up in their faeces. The DNA also allowed scientists to reconstruct genomes of extinct animals, offering new information about ancient biodiversity.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to analyze the woolly mammoth DNA more closely and continue studying other frozen burrows. This could help build a clearer picture of how Arctic ecosystems changed over hundreds of thousands of years and how animals adapted to past climate changes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Frozen Arctic ground squirrel droppings have preserved DNA that reveals a detailed picture of an ancient ecosystem dating back 700,000 years.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les crottes de tamias gelées révèlent un écosystème arctique vieux de 700 000 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient souvent les écosystèmes anciens grâce aux fossiles et aux os. Récemment, ils ont découvert que les excréments gelés des tamias arctiques peuvent aussi conserver l’ADN de nombreuses espèces ayant vécu il y a des centaines de milliers d’années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Tyler Murchie a étudié des crottes gelées, appelées coprolithes, de tamias arctiques trouvés au Yukon, au Canada. Ces terriers étaient gelés dans le pergélisol et datent de 30 000 à 700 000 ans. En extrayant l’ADN de ces petites crottes, les scientifiques ont identifié plus de 200 groupes de plantes et de nombreux animaux, dont des mammouths laineux, des chevaux, des bisons, des loups et de grands félins.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est unique car elle offre un aperçu détaillé d’un riche écosystème arctique ancien grâce à une source inattendue : les crottes de tamias. Elle montre que ces tamias mangeaient une grande variété d’aliments, y compris de la viande, ce qui explique la présence d’ADN d’animaux de grande taille dans leurs excréments. L’ADN a aussi permis de reconstruire les génomes d’animaux disparus, fournissant de nouvelles informations sur la biodiversité ancienne.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’analyser plus précisément l’ADN des mammouths laineux et de continuer à étudier d’autres terriers gelés. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre comment les écosystèmes arctiques ont changé sur des centaines de milliers d’années et comment les animaux se sont adaptés aux changements climatiques passés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les crottes gelées de tamias arctiques ont conservé de l’ADN révélant un écosystème ancien détaillé datant de 700 000 ans.
articles.personality_title_es: Excrementos congelados de ardillas revelan ecosistema ártico de hace 700,000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen estudiar ecosistemas antiguos mediante fósiles y huesos. Recientemente, descubrieron que los excrementos congelados de ardillas árticas también pueden conservar ADN de muchas especies que vivieron hace cientos de miles de años.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Tyler Murchie estudió excrementos congelados, llamados coprolitos, de ardillas árticas encontrados en Yukon, Canadá. Estas madrigueras estaban congeladas en permafrost y datan de entre 30,000 y 700,000 años. Al extraer ADN de estos pequeños excrementos, los científicos identificaron más de 200 grupos de plantas y muchos animales, incluidos mamuts lanudos, caballos, bisontes, lobos y grandes felinos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es único porque ofrece una mirada detallada a un ecosistema ártico antiguo usando una fuente inesperada: excrementos de ardillas. Muestra que estas ardillas comían una gran variedad de alimentos, incluso carne, lo que explica cómo el ADN de animales grandes llegó a sus excrementos. El ADN también permitió reconstruir genomas de animales extintos, ofreciendo nueva información sobre la biodiversidad antigua.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean analizar más a fondo el ADN de los mamuts lanudos y seguir estudiando otras madrigueras congeladas. Esto podría ayudar a entender mejor cómo cambiaron los ecosistemas árticos durante cientos de miles de años y cómo los animales se adaptaron a los cambios climáticos pasados.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los excrementos congelados de ardillas árticas conservaron ADN que revela un ecosistema antiguo detallado de hace 700,000 años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an ancient Arctic ground squirrel’s burrow cross-section, showing layers of frozen faecal pellets embedded in permafrost, surrounded by faint, ghostly silhouettes of woolly mammoths, bison, horses, and big cats subtly intertwined with plant roots and insects, symbolizing the preserved DNA and the rich, interconnected ice-age ecosystem beneath the earth.

articles.title: How pigeons find their way home: the answer is a magnetic compass in the liver
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM For decades, researchers looked for the seat of magnetoreception in all the obvious places: the eyes, the inner ear, the beak. A study just published in Science points somewhere none of them expected. The organ doing the magnetic navigation work in homing pigeons appears to be the liver. Iron […]
The post How pigeons find their way home: the answer is a magnetic compass in the liver first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

For decades, researchers looked for the seat of magnetoreception in all the obvious places: the eyes, the inner ear, the beak. A study just published in Science points somewhere none of them expected. The organ doing the magnetic navigation work in homing pigeons appears to be the liver.

Iron accumulation with a purpose

The cells involved are macrophages. Their usual job is to break down old red blood cells, accumulating iron as they go. Researchers at the University of Bonn found dense concentrations of these iron-laden macrophages in pigeon liver tissue, pressed against nerve fibers. “This actually makes it very likely that [the nerve cells and macrophages] are communicating,” says Clivia Lisowski, the immunologist who led the study.

The navigation angle came from a conference coffee break. Immunologist Christian Kurts had been studying the iron buildup in macrophages when he ran into animal behaviorist Martin Wikelski. “We had this eureka moment,” Kurts says. “Maybe we test whether these cells are involved.”

So they did. The team trained 34 pigeons on a 12-mile route through the German Alps, then experimentally disabled the liver macrophages in some of the birds. On overcast days, those pigeons couldn’t get home. In clear weather, they were fine, navigating by the sun instead. The liver appears to cover the magnetic side of things. The sky covers the rest.

Two compasses, not one

“The sense of magnetism has been a mystery for a century, and nobody could solve where that sits and how that works,” says Wikelski, a director at the Max Planck Institute of Animal Behavior. “Now, we think we have found, really, a workable solution.”

Not everyone is ready to call it solved. Thorsten Ritz, a biophysicist at UC Irvine, has spent years building the case that songbirds detect Earth’s magnetic field using light-sensitive molecules in the eye, a different mechanism with its own supporting evidence. He thinks the field should stay open. “There are almost always multiple solutions to how an animal can get an evolutionary advantage,” he says. “I am in favor of keeping an open mind rather than trying to find winners and losers.”

Researchers Simon Spiro and Hal Drakesmith, writing alongside the study in Science, think both camps may be right about different things. “Perhaps one process dominates for long-distance navigation, whereas another is used for more specific destination-finding,” they write.

What comes next

Whether sea turtles, gray whales, and spiny lobsters use the same iron-macrophage system is now an open question. The team still needs to trace which nerve pathways carry the signal from the liver to the brain, and which parts of the brain do something with it. “Every little bit of it that we can resolve in the future will of course substantiate the validity of the findings,” Kurts says.

Evolution tends to find the same solution independently more often than seems reasonable. Whether this is one of those cases or not is still unclear.

Source study: Science– Homing pigeon navigation relies on superparamagnetic macrophages under overcast conditions

articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about how pigeons navigate using a magnetic compass located in the liver, which solves a century-old mystery of magnetoreception. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding animal navigation and could inspire further research in biology and technology. The article provides detailed context, experimental evidence, and discusses future research directions, indicating substantive and meaningful impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover pigeons use a magnetic compass in their liver to navigate home
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, scientists have been trying to understand how pigeons find their way home using Earth's magnetic field. They looked in expected places like the eyes, ears, and beak but found no clear answers.

**What happened** – A new study from the University of Bonn found that pigeons use special cells in their liver for magnetic navigation. These cells, called macrophages, hold iron and are connected to nerve fibers. Researchers disabled these liver cells in some pigeons and found that on cloudy days, these birds couldn’t navigate properly, while on sunny days they could still use the sun to find their way.

**Impact** – This discovery solves a long-standing mystery about how pigeons sense magnetic fields. It shows that the liver, not just the eyes or brain, plays an important role in navigation. The finding suggests animals might use more than one compass to find their way, combining magnetic sensing with other methods like using the sun.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to find out if other animals, like sea turtles or whales, also use iron-rich cells in their liver for navigation. They also aim to trace how signals from the liver reach the brain and how the brain uses this information to guide movement.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Pigeons use a magnetic compass in their liver to navigate home, revealing a surprising new way animals sense Earth's magnetic field.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pigeons utilisent une boussole magnétique dans leur foie pour retrouver leur chemin
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment les pigeons retrouvent leur chemin grâce au champ magnétique terrestre. Ils ont cherché dans les endroits habituels comme les yeux, les oreilles et le bec, sans trouver de réponse claire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude de l'université de Bonn a découvert que les pigeons utilisent des cellules spéciales dans leur foie pour s'orienter. Ces cellules, appelées macrophages, contiennent du fer et sont reliées à des fibres nerveuses. Les chercheurs ont désactivé ces cellules dans certains pigeons et ont constaté que par temps couvert, ces oiseaux ne pouvaient plus s'orienter correctement, alors que par temps ensoleillé, ils pouvaient encore utiliser le soleil.

**Impact** – Cette découverte résout un mystère ancien sur la façon dont les pigeons perçoivent les champs magnétiques. Elle montre que le foie, et non seulement les yeux ou le cerveau, joue un rôle important dans la navigation. Cela suggère que les animaux peuvent utiliser plus d'une boussole pour se repérer, combinant la détection magnétique avec d'autres méthodes comme l'orientation au soleil.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques souhaitent savoir si d'autres animaux, comme les tortues de mer ou les baleines, utilisent aussi des cellules riches en fer dans leur foie pour s'orienter. Ils veulent aussi comprendre comment les signaux du foie arrivent au cerveau et comment ce dernier utilise ces informations pour guider les déplacements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les pigeons utilisent une boussole magnétique dans leur foie pour retrouver leur chemin, révélant une nouvelle façon surprenante dont les animaux perçoivent le champ magnétique terrestre.
articles.personality_title_es: Los científicos descubren que las palomas usan una brújula magnética en el hígado para regresar a casa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los científicos intentaron entender cómo las palomas encuentran el camino a casa usando el campo magnético de la Tierra. Buscaron en lugares comunes como los ojos, los oídos y el pico, pero no encontraron respuestas claras.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio de la Universidad de Bonn descubrió que las palomas usan células especiales en su hígado para la navegación magnética. Estas células, llamadas macrófagos, contienen hierro y están conectadas a fibras nerviosas. Los investigadores desactivaron estas células en algunas palomas y encontraron que en días nublados no podían navegar bien, pero en días soleados podían usar el sol para orientarse.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento resuelve un misterio antiguo sobre cómo las palomas detectan los campos magnéticos. Muestra que el hígado, no solo los ojos o el cerebro, tiene un papel importante en la navegación. Esto sugiere que los animales pueden usar más de una brújula para orientarse, combinando la detección magnética con otros métodos como usar el sol.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren saber si otros animales, como las tortugas marinas o las ballenas, también usan células con hierro en el hígado para navegar. También buscan descubrir cómo las señales del hígado llegan al cerebro y cómo el cerebro utiliza esta información para guiar el movimiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Las palomas usan una brújula magnética en su hígado para regresar a casa, revelando una forma sorprendente en que los animales perciben el campo magnético de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a homing pigeon soaring gently over the German Alps under a soft sky, with a translucent, glowing outline of a liver inside the pigeon filled with shimmering clusters of iron-rich cells connected by delicate nerve-like threads, symbolizing the bird’s internal magnetic compass guiding its flight home.
